title: Apply: Amending the Constitution
description: Apply your understanding of process to amend the Constitution as written in Article V.
topic_path: Civil Dialogue, The Constitution, and Constitutional Interpretation

assessment_items.question_content:
Janae has a new amendment she wants to add to the Constitution that would grant 16-year-olds the right to vote. Two-thirds of Congress has voted in favor of the proposal. The amendment now needs approval from the states to be ratified.

**How many states need to approve the amendment before it can be ratified?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt of Article V of the U.S. Constitution.**

The Congress, whenever two thirds of both Houses shall deem it necessary, shall propose Amendments to this Constitution, or, on the Application of the Legislatures of two thirds of the several States, shall call a Convention for proposing Amendments, which, in either Case, shall be valid to all Intents and Purposes, as Part of this Constitution, when ratified by the Legislatures of three fourths of the several States, or by Conventions in three fourths thereof, as the one or the other Mode of Ratification may be proposed by the Congress; Provided that no Amendment which may be made prior to the Year One thousand eight hundred and eight shall in any Manner affect the first and fourth Clauses in the Ninth Section of the first Article; and that no State, without its Consent, shall be deprived of its equal Suffrage in the Senate.

**What process does this part of the Constitution describe?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: How to change the Constitution
How to remove the president from office
How to elect a new president to power
How a bill becomes a law

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt of George Mason’s speech at the Constitutional Convention.**

Col. Mason urged the necessity of such a provision. The plan now to be formed will certainly
be defective, as the Confederation has been found on trial to be. Amendments therefore will be
necessary, and it will be better to provide for them, in an easy, regular and Constitutional way
than to trust to chance and violence. It would be improper to require the consent of the [National]
Legislature, because they may abuse their power, and refuse their consent on that very account.
The opportunity for such an abuse, may be the fault of the Constitution calling for [amendment].

**According to the excerpt, which recommendation does Mason make about future changes to the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The process should be difficult as amendments should be rare.
The process should be simple so that it reflects the will of the people.
The process should rely heavily on congressional approval and not on the states.
The process should mirror the process in the Articles of Confederation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from George Mason’s speech at the Constitutional Convention.**

Col. Mason urged the necessity of such a provision. The plan now to be formed will certainly
be defective, as the Confederation has been found on trial to be. Amendments therefore will be
necessary, and it will be better to provide for them, in an easy, regular and Constitutional way
than to trust to chance and violence. It would be improper to require the consent of the [National]
Legislature, because they may abuse their power, and refuse their consent on that very account.
The opportunity for such an abuse, may be the fault of the Constitution calling for [amendment].

**Why does Mason think amendments will be necessary?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Because the Constitutional Convention was held in secret without state approval.
Because the new structure of government would most likely lead to an abuse of power by Congress.
Because the Constitutional Convention did not have a single recommendation.
Because the Constitution would have flaws, just like the Articles of Confederation did.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 article in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer.***

Many of the advocates for the new Constitution are transported, by an immediate [[☃ definition 1]], beyond the bounds of reason and [[☃ definition 2]] not to assert that it has neither faults nor imperfections…

The Convention were not possessed of arrogance enough to think this, more than any other human work, entirely free from faults, and, therefore, have provided for its future amendment, in such particulars as the sense of the people may, at any time, require.

**According to the excerpt, why did the framers include an amendment process to the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a moral concern that keeps you from doing something

choices.content: Because the Constitution would inevitably have flaws
Because the framers knew that the new Constitution would be controversial
Because the Constitution was designed to be replaced every ten years
Because the framers knew the system of government didn’t work

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Thomas Jefferson’s 1787 letter to Monseiur Dumas.**

Our Federal Convention is likely to sit till October; there is a general disposition through the States to adopt what they shall propose, and we may be assured their propositions will be wise, as a more able assembly never sat in America. Happily for us, that when we find our constitutions defective and insufficient to secure the happiness of our people, we can assemble with all the coolness of philosophers, and set it to rights, while every other nation on earth must have [[☃ definition 1]] to arms to amend or to restore their constitutions.

**Which part of the Constitution gives the people the power to “set it to rights,” as Jefferson describes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: using something to help achieve a goal

choices.content: Article I
Article III
Article V
Article VII

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Thomas Jefferson’s 1787 letter to Monseiur Dumas.**

Our Federal Convention is likely to sit till October; there is a general disposition through the States to adopt what they shall propose, and we may be assured their propositions will be wise, as a more able assembly never sat in America. Happily for us, that when we find our constitutions defective and insufficient to secure the happiness of our people, we can assemble with all the coolness of philosophers, and set it to rights, while every other nation on earth must have [[☃ definition 1]] to arms to amend or to restore their constitutions.

**According to Jefferson, how is the American system of government different from systems in other countries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: using something to help achieve a goal

choices.content: In the United States, the government focuses on the people’s happiness; in other countries, the government focuses on the public good.
In the United States, the constitution is defective and flawed; in other countries, constitutions are free from flaws.
In the United States, the people can calmly amend their constitution; in other countries, people must use violence  to change their government.
In the United States, philosophers ensure that the government’s laws are moral; in other countries, people who develop the laws aren’t as wise.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 43, written by James Madison in 1788.**

That useful alterations will be suggested by experience, could not but be [[☃ definition 1]]. It was [[☃ definition 2]], therefore, that a mode for introducing them should be provided. The mode preferred by the convention seems to be stamped with every mark of [[☃ definition 3]]. It guards equally against that extreme [[☃ definition 4]], which would [[☃ definition 5]] the Constitution too [[☃ definition 6]]; and that extreme difficulty, which might [[☃ definition 7]] its discovered faults. It, moreover, equally enables the general and the State governments to originate the amendment of errors, as they may be pointed out by the experience on one side, or on the other.

**This excerpt most directly reflects which statement about the framers?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an ability to do something easily

choices.content: The framers opposed the Constitution as it was and would agree to ratify the Constitution only if a bill of rights was added.
The framers wanted an amendment process that would allow changes to the Constitution but be difficult enough to make amendments uncommon.
The framers wanted the national legislature to own the amendment process, as they believed state governments would request too many changes.
The framers opposed the inclusion of a formal amendment process, as the Constitution created a perfect system of government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 43, written by James Madison in 1788.**

That useful alterations will be suggested by experience, could not but be [[☃ definition 1]]. It was [[☃ definition 2]], therefore, that a mode for introducing them should be provided. The mode preferred by the convention seems to be stamped with every mark of [[☃ definition 3]]. It guards equally against that extreme [[☃ definition 4]], which would [[☃ definition 5]] the Constitution too [[☃ definition 6]]; and that extreme difficulty, which might [[☃ definition 7]] its discovered faults. It, moreover, equally enables the general and the State governments to originate the amendment of errors, as they may be pointed out by the experience on one side, or on the other.

**Which statement best summarizes Madison’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an ability to do something easily

choices.content: The Constitution does include an amendment process, but the process is so difficult that amendments will never happen.
The Constitution is not meant to exist forever, and it is expected that future generations will replace it frequently.
The Constitution is as perfect as it can be, so there will be no need to add amendments in the future.
The Constitution will need to be amended, and the process to add amendments should be neither too easy or too difficult.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The Documentary History of the Ratification of the Constitution* (2019) edited by John P. Kaminski, Gaspare J. Saladino, Richard Leffler, Charles H. Schoenleber and Margaret A. Hogan.**

[[☃ definition 1]] declared if no change should be made in this part of the plan, he should be obliged to dissent from the whole of it. He had from the beginning he said been convinced that radical changes in the system of the Union were necessary. Under this [[☃ definition 2]] he had brought forward a set of republican propositions as the basis and outline of a reform. These Republican propositions had however, much to his regret been widely, and in his opinion, [[☃ definition 3]] departed from—In this state of things it was his idea and he accordingly meant to propose, that the State Conventions should be at liberty to offer amendments to the plan,—and that these should be submitted to a second General Convention, with full power to settle the Constitution finally— He did not expect to succeed in this proposition, but the [[☃ definition 4]] of his duty in making the attempt, would give quiet to his own mind.

**Randolph’s sentiments, as expressed in the excerpt, are most similar to those of which group?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: Edmund Randolph, a delegate from Virginia
in such a way that it is impossible to come to agreement
the act of accomplishing or completing something

choices.content: The Anti-Federalists
The Federalists
The Loyalists
The Whigs

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The Documentary History of the Ratification of the Constitution* (2019) edited by John P. Kaminski, Gaspare J. Saladino, Richard Leffler, Charles H. Schoenleber and Margaret A. Hogan.**

[[☃ definition 1]] declared if no change should be made in this part of the plan, he should be obliged to dissent from the whole of it. He had from the beginning he said been convinced that radical changes in the system of the Union were necessary. Under this [[☃ definition 2]] he had brought forward a set of republican propositions as the basis and outline of a reform. These Republican propositions had however, much to his regret been widely, and in his opinion, [[☃ definition 3]] departed from—In this state of things it was his idea and he accordingly meant to propose, that the State Conventions should be at liberty to offer amendments to the plan,—and that these should be submitted to a second General Convention, with full power to settle the Constitution finally— He did not expect to succeed in this proposition, but the [[☃ definition 4]] of his duty in making the attempt, would give quiet to his own mind.

**Arguments like Randolph’s most directly led to the inclusion of which part of the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: Edmund Randolph, a delegate from Virginia
in such a way that it is impossible to come to agreement
the act of accomplishing or completing something

choices.content: Article I
Article II
Article IV
Article V

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a pamphlet titled “The genuine principles of the ancient saxon, or english, constitution, Philadelphia, 1776.” The author used the [[☃ definition 1]] Demophilus.**

There cannot be a more dangerous doctrine in a state, than to admit that the legislative power has a right to alter the constitution. For as the constitution limits the authority of the legislature, if the legislature can alter the constitution, they can give themselves what bounds they please.

**How does the amendment process, outlined in Article V, address the concerns in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Supreme Court has the power to strike down proposed amendments as unconstitutional.
The ratification step requires passage by either three-fourths of state legislatures or special state ratifying conventions.
Two-thirds of both houses of Congress must approve to ratify a new amendment.
The president has ultimate veto power over any proposed amendments.
